DearLavion/About.title: About
DearLavion/About.content:
✅ How Your App Is Unique
Your concept combines help‑based social matching + travel context + micro‑wish fulfilment in a way that isn’t exactly replicated by existing travel or help‑based platforms. Features that make it unique include:
🎯 Dual roles with empathy focus (Wishers & Copilots)
Most travel or help apps either connect travelers with locals or match people seeking tasks with helpers — your idea pairs travel needs with actual kindness or skills offered by others in a community setting.
✈️ On‑the‑fly help for travel pain points
Your model isn’t just about social networking or travel planning — it’s about fulfilling small actionable travel wishes (e.g., delivering items, giving directions, helping with errands) which combines social support + utility.
🌍 Travel first, help next
Rather than being a generic task marketplace or travel planner, your platform is built around solving travel frustrations through human connection, which positions it differently in the ecosystem.

DearLavion/Social-Media/Social-Media-Str.title: Social Media Strategy
DearLavion/Social-Media/Social-Media-Str.content:
1️⃣ Core Social Media Strategy
Goals


Awareness: Introduce Dear Lavion as the go-to app for travel help & small wishes.


Engagement: Encourage users to share travel frustrations, stories, and wishes.


Conversion: Drive downloads/sign-ups for Wishers and Copilots.


Target Audience


Travelers aged 18–35 (solo travelers, backpackers, urban explorers).


Tech-savvy people looking for stress-free travel solutions.


Socially conscious people who enjoy helping others or receiving help.


Content Pillars


Pain Points: Highlight common travel struggles (lost, crowded areas, language barriers, luggage, missed events).


Solutions: Show how Copilots make life easier and fun.


Emotions: Heartwarming stories of kindness, friendship, and travel happiness.


Social Proof: Showcase user testimonials, numbers of wishes granted, and community impact.


Tips & Hacks: Travel tips, micro-guides, and seasonal hacks.


Engagement Posts: Polls, “Would you…?”, or choose-your-adventure style posts.



2️⃣ Content Formats for TikTok & Instagram

navigate a city like a local with Dear Lavion”Before/After / Split-ScreenShow problem vs solutionSolo traveler lost / guided by CopilotUser Testimonials / ReactionsSocial proofWisher video describing DearLavion/Social-Media/Visual-Storyboar.title: Visual Storyboard
DearLavion/Social-Media/Visual-Storyboar.content:
✅ Storyboard Notes


Duration: Each Reel/Story ~15–30 seconds.


Branding: Keep colors, fonts, and animation style consistent.


Animation Style:


Dotted paths for guidance.


Glow or pop-up effects for solutions and highlights.


Subtle shake or zoom for pain points.




Text Overlays: Short, punchy, easy to read in 2–3 seconds per line.


Music / Sounds: Use soft upbeat music for solutions/emotions, slightly tense music for pain points.


Stickers / CTA: Polls for engagement, hearts for emotional moments, “Join Today” for conversion.

DearLavion/Year-Plan.title: Year Plan
DearLavion/Year-Plan.content: MonthThemePurposeJanIntroducing Dear Lavion & Pain PointsHook audience, show struggles travelers face, why your app existsFebWisher StoriesShowcase real or simulated Wisher pain points and wishesMarCopilot StoriesHighlight Copilots solving problems, empathy in actionAprCommunity ImpactShow community growth, statistics, ripple effectMayTips & Hacks for TravelersPosition Dear Lavion as helpful and educationalJunUser-Generated ContentShare Wisher/Copilot experiences and testimonialsJulBehind the Scenes / Founder’s StoryHumanize the brand, show passion and missionAugChallenges / Interactive ContentQuizzes, polls, engagement campaignsSepFeature FocusHighlight app features, new updates, upcoming releasesOctEmotional StoriesFocus on travel struggles and resolutionsNovSeasonal / Festive WishesTie content to holidays, seasons, and special campaignsDecYear in Review / Community HighlightsCelebrate impact, thank users, promote joining next year

General-Knowledge/AI/1.-Artificial-Intel.title: 1. Artificial Intelligence (AI)
General-Knowledge/AI/1.-Artificial-Intel.content:
What

AI is the broad field, ability of machines to mimic human intelligence: such as reasoning, decision-making, and understanding language.

Why

To automate decision-making, reduce human effort, and solve complex problems at scale.

How

Through rules, algorithms, and data-driven models—including machine learning.

One-Line Differentiation (Very Interview-Friendly)

AI: Thinks like a human
ML: Learns from past data
DL: Learns deeply using neural networks
GenAI: Creates new content
LLM: Understands and generates language


┌─────────────────────────────┐
│ Artificial Intelligence     │
│  - Decision making          │
│  - Reasoning                │
└─────────────┬───────────────┘
              ▼
┌─────────────────────────────┐
│ Machine Learning            │
│  - Learns from data         │
└─────────────┬───────────────┘
              ▼
┌─────────────────────────────┐
│ Deep Learning               │
│  - Neural networks          │
│  - Large datasets           │
└─────────────┬───────────────┘
              ▼
┌─────────────────────────────┐
│ Generative AI               │
│  - Creates new content      │
└─────────────┬───────────────┘
              ▼
┌─────────────────────────────┐
│ Large Language Models       │
│  - Text & code generation   │
└─────────────────────────────┘

General-Knowledge/AI/2.-Machine-Learning.title: 2. Machine Learning (ML)
General-Knowledge/AI/2.-Machine-Learning.content:
What

ML is a subset of AI where systems learn patterns from data instead of being explicitly programmed.

Why

To make systems adaptive and accurate when rules are too complex to hard-code.

How

By training models on historical data and using them to make predictions.

Example: spam detection, recommendation systems.

General-Knowledge/AI/3.-Deep-Learning-(D.title: 3. Deep Learning (DL)
General-Knowledge/AI/3.-Deep-Learning-(D.content:
What

DL is a subset of ML that uses multi-layer neural networks to learn complex patterns.

Why

To handle unstructured data like images, audio, and text at scale.

How

Using neural networks with many layers trained on large datasets using GPUs.

Example: image recognition, speech-to-text.

General-Knowledge/AI/4.-Generative-AI-(G.title: 4. Generative AI (Gen AI)
General-Knowledge/AI/4.-Generative-AI-(G.content:
What

Generative AI is a class of models that create new content such as text, images, code, or audio.

Why

To automate creativity and content generation, not just prediction.

How

By learning the probability distribution of data and generating new outputs that resemble it.

Example: ChatGPT, image generators, code assistants.

General-Knowledge/AI/5.-Large-Language-M.title: 5. Large Language Model (LLM)
General-Knowledge/AI/5.-Large-Language-M.content:
What

LLMs are a type of Generative AI specialized in understanding and generating human language.

Why

To enable natural language interaction, reasoning, summarization, and code generation.

How

Using large transformer-based neural networks trained on massive text datasets.

Example: GPT, Claude, Gemini.

General-Knowledge/AI/Oracle-AI.title: Oracle AI
General-Knowledge/AI/Oracle-AI.content:
What
Ability of machines to mimic human intelligence: cognitive and problem solving

Machine Learning


Deep Learning

Generative AI



LLM
probabilistic model of text
attempt to produce the next series of words that are most likely to follow from the previous

General-Knowledge/Devops/1.-What-is-Devo.title: 1. What is Devops?
General-Knowledge/Devops/1.-What-is-Devo.content:
What
DevOps is an organizational and technical approach that optimizes software delivery by connecting development and operations teams to unify the infrastructure.
Development + Operations = Unifies Infrastructure
Why
It emphasizes automation, observability, continuous delivery, and reliability engineering to enable rapid, safe, and scalable deployments.
How
Real-World Example (Dear Lavion)

“When I push to the develop branch, GitHub Actions builds and tests the app, packages it as a Docker image, and pushes it to GitHub Container Registry. Then Ansible connects to my dev server over SSH, pulls the image from the registry, and runs the container. This ensures consistent, automated, and repeatable deployments.”

┌────────────────────────────┐
│         Developer          │
│                            │
│   git push (develop)       │
└──────────────┬─────────────┘
               │
               ▼
┌────────────────────────────┐
│     GitHub Repository      │
│                            │
│   Source Code              │
└──────────────┬─────────────┘
               │
               ▼
┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
│        GitHub Actions (CI)              │
│                                        │
│  - Build application                   │
│  - Build Docker image                  │
│  - Push image to registry              │
└──────────────┬─────────────────────────┘
               │
               ▼
┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  GitHub Container Registry (GHCR)       │
│                                        │
│  Immutable Docker Images                │
└──────────────┬─────────────────────────┘
               │
               │  Ansible (CD over SSH)
               ▼
┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
│            DEV Server                  │
│                                        │
│  - docker pull                          │
│  - docker stop / run                    │
└──────────────┬─────────────────────────┘
               │
               ▼
┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
│   Authentication Service (Running)     │
│                                        │
│   Exposed to internal / test traffic   │
└────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explain This Diagram (Interview)

“CI ends when the Docker image is pushed to the registry. CD begins when Ansible pulls that image and deploys it to the dev server.”

Tie It Directly to Your Setup (Very Strong)

“In my workflow, Ansible connects to the dev server over SSH, pulls the Docker image from the registry, stops the old container, and starts the new one—so deployments are consistent and automated.”

Oracle cloud CI/CD Architecture

General-Knowledge/Devops/2.-What-is-Ansi.title: 2. What is Ansible?
General-Knowledge/Devops/2.-What-is-Ansi.content:
What Ansible does
One-Sentence Interview Answer (Very Strong)

“Ansible is a general-purpose automation tool used for configuration management, application deployment, and operational orchestration—not just server deployments.”

Tie It Back to Your Use (Important)

“In my case, I primarily use Ansible for deployment automation, but the same playbooks approach can manage configuration, scaling, and operational tasks.”

Why I use Ansible

“It allows me to deploy consistently without manually logging into the server, reducing human error.”

How I use Ansible

“After GitHub Actions pushes the Docker image to the registry, Ansible connects to the dev server over SSH, pulls the image, and restarts the container.”

General-Knowledge/Java/1.-What--is-Java.title: 1. What  is Java
General-Knowledge/Java/1.-What--is-Java.content:
Java is a platform-independent, object-oriented programming language that runs on the JVM and is commonly used for backend, enterprise, and Android development.
Platform-independent (how to explain it)
What it means

Platform-independent means Java programs can run on different operating systems without changing the code.

How it works (key point interviewers want)

Java code is compiled into bytecode, not machine code.
That bytecode runs on the Java Virtual Machine (JVM), and each OS has its own JVM implementation.

One-line example

Write once, run anywhere — as long as a JVM exists.

Strong interview phrasing

Java is platform-independent because it compiles to bytecode, which is executed by the JVM rather than the operating system directly.

Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) in Java
What it means

Object-oriented programming organizes code around objects that represent real-world entities and behavior.

It helps make our code reusable, organized, and easier to maintain which is important for large scale systems by following OOP concepts
The 4 pillars (say them confidently)


Encapsulation –  is the practice of protecting and organizing code inside a class.


Inheritance – reuse and extend existing code


Polymorphism – adds flexibility- allows objects to take multiple forms;


Abstraction – hide complexity, expose only what’s needed


Strong interview phrasing

Java uses OOP principles to make code modular, reusable, and easier to maintain, which is important for large-scale systems.

Class vs Object

A class is a blueprint or template that defines properties and behaviors, while an object is a real instance of that class created at runtime.

General-Knowledge/Java/2.-Why-Java-is-Po.filePath: General Knowledge/Java/2. Why Java is Popular.md
General-Knowledge/Java/2.-Why-Java-is-Po.title: 2. Why Java is Popular
General-Knowledge/Java/2.-Why-Java-is-Po.content:
Java is popular because it’s platform-independent, strongly typed, and object-oriented. In addition, it has a huge ecosystem with frameworks like Spring Boot and Hibernate, which provide powerful tools and abstractions that allow developers to solve problems faster without reinventing the wheel.


Platform independent – runs on any device with a JVM (Java Virtual Machine)


Strongly typed & safe – reduces runtime errors - Errors caught at compile time


Object-oriented – organized and scalable code


Huge ecosystem – Faster development, proven libraries - frameworks like Spring Boot, Hibernate


Very stable – heavily used in finance, telecom, government

General-Knowledge/Java/3.-How-Java-Works.title: 3. How Java Works
General-Knowledge/Java/3.-How-Java-Works.content:
You write Java code → MyApp.java


Java compiler converts it to bytecode


The JVM runs that bytecode on any OS


Java code → Bytecode → JVM → Any OS

General-Knowledge/Java/5.-Java-Modifiers.title: 5. Java Modifiers
General-Knowledge/Java/5.-Java-Modifiers.content:
What

Modifiers are keywords in Java that change the behavior or accessibility of classes, methods, and variables.


Access modifiers control visibility: public, protected, private, and package-private (default).


Non-access modifiers change behavior: static, final, abstract, synchronized, volatile, etc.




Access Modifiers
What:

Control visibility of classes, methods, and variables.


public → accessible everywhere


private → accessible only inside the class


protected → accessible in subclasses and same package


default (no keyword) → accessible only in the same package



Why:

To protect data, enforce encapsulation, and control which parts of code can access members.

Non-Access Modifiers in Java
What

Non-access modifiers change the behavior or properties of classes, methods, or variables without affecting visibility.
Examples: static, final, abstract, synchronized, volatile, transient, native.


Why

They allow you to:



Define shared data (static)


Create constants (final)


Specify abstract behavior (abstract)


Ensure thread safety (synchronized, volatile)


Optimize or interact with JVM (transient, native)


In short: they control functionality, not access.
How

Static → belongs to the class, not instances

class Counter {     static int count = 0;  // Shared among all objects }

Final → constant value, cannot be changed

final double PI = 3.1416;

Abstract → defines a method/class without implementation

abstract class Shape {     abstract void draw(); }

Synchronized → thread-safe method

synchronized void increment() { count++; }

General-Knowledge/Java/Random-Questions/.filePath: General Knowledge/Java/Random Questions/1. Why does Java allow only single inheritance of classes (one parent → child)?.md
General-Knowledge/Java/Random-Questions/.title: 1. Why does Java allow only single inheritance of classes (one parent → child)?
General-Knowledge/Java/Random-Questions/.content:
Java allows a class to extend only one parent class to avoid the diamond problem
Occurs when multiple parent classes have methods with the same signature.
Single inheritance keeps the class hierarchy simple and predictable, reduces ambiguity, and makes code easier to maintain.
“Java solves multiple inheritance using interfaces instead”

General-Knowledge/System-Design/Caching.title: Caching
General-Knowledge/System-Design/Caching.content:
What
temporary storage that keeps recently used data handy or close by (get it much faster next time)
Why

to prevent directly accessing the slower data source
the gap adds up quickly when serving thousands of requests
It keeps copies of frequently used data in a faster layer (often times memory but not always) so that system don’t have to reach all the back way to the slower source


Where to cache?


External caching
use dedicated caching service (own server and has own memory)



In-process caching
fast, since on the same server as application



CDN  (Content Delivery Network)
cache content closest to the user



Client Side Caching
Ex. Strava



Cache Architecture

Cache Aside
most used

Write through

Write Behind

Read Through
mostly used by CDN, on step2 needs to have a special caching library


Cache Eviction Policies

Least Recently Used (LRU)
evict the not recently used data
Least Frequently Used
evict the least often used data
FIFO
TTL
each item expires

References
www.youtube.com/watch

General-Knowledge/System-Design/Kafka.title: Kafka
General-Knowledge/System-Design/Kafka.content:
open-source distributed event streaming platform
What

Message Queue -  a system where messages are stored until they are consumed. Each message is typically processed by only one consumer.
Ideal for task delivery guarantees that only needs to be processed once
Stream - continuous flow of data that can be processed in real time by multiple consumers.
- multiple independent consumers that process the same data differently (e.g., one consumer updates a dashboard, another updates a database).
- Pub-Sub (can be multiple subscribers), ideal for analytics

Why Kafka?

Fault Tolerant
a. Leader-Follower Partitions for backup
b. Retry and error
High Availability
Can easily scale by
1. Adding more Brokers (physical servers)
2. Good partition key design - to evenly distribute load

Flow
A. Producer send message and specify the topic and partition
Topic - Logical group of partitions/data, can have multiple partition

Partition - "queue" of ordered immutable sequence of messages
	- Kafka strategy to scale as they allow for messages to be consumed in parallel.

B. Broker / Server receives the message
C. Consumer subscribes to the topic
With consumer groups, each event is guaranteed to only be processed by one consumer in the group.

Important:
1. Consumer internally track the offset where it stopped
2. Once consumer consumes the message it responds to the broker the offset “I finished reading offset 99”
3. Broker also keeps track of the offset for backup purposes incase consumer is down
Optimization Strategy

Producer can batch send message
Producer can compress message before sending

Logs Retention Policy
Defaults to 7 days
 When log file reaches 1 GB

Reference
www.hellointerview.com/learn/system-design/deep-dives/kafka
www.youtube.com/watch

General-Knowledge/Tools/Github-Page-=-Qu.title: Github Page = Quartz + Obsidian
General-Knowledge/Tools/Github-Page-=-Qu.content:
About
Repository dearlavion.github.io/ is generated by quartz
All obsidian generated notes are maintained in main branch
Local Test:
cd quartz/
yarn run quartz build --serve

To update deploy gitHub page:


Generate files in main branch first
cd quartz/
yarn run quartz build


Generated new files are in /public
Note: remove local gh-pages branch
git branch -D gh-pages
cd quartz/public
git init
git remote add origin github.com/dearlavion/dearlavion.github.io.git
git checkout -b gh-pages
git add .
git commit -m “Update site”
git push -f origin gh-pages —force
Note: github might take time to deploy updated page
